metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the plan-review permissive-invariant test to assert on the correct predicate: lock that the FROZEN SET of plan-review criteria with `default_posture == 'blocking'` matches a known-good baseline (e.g. the set `{F1, E2, ...}` confirmed as pre-existing), so any new `default_posture: 'blocking'` entry added by the impact redesign is caught — not the vacuously-true `blocking_enabled` absence check.
findings.finding: The permissive-invariant locking test for plan-review asserts the wrong predicate. Plan-review's `threshold_for` resolver (in `criteria/model.py`) derives blocking from `default_posture == 'blocking'`, not from `blocking_enabled`. The proposed test — 'NO plan-review criterion carries `blocking_enabled: true`' — is vacuously true (the field is never present in `criteria_routing.json` for plan-review) and would pass even if a newly impact-redesigned criterion accidentally received `default_posture: 'blocking'`. The meaningful invariant — that the impact redesign added no NEW criterion with `default_posture: 'blocking'` — is not locked.
findings.location: Section A ('Permissive rollout') / AC line 3 — 'PLAN-REVIEW carries NO `blocking_enabled: true` on any criterion'
findings.suggested_fix: In `tests/unit/test_impact_model_versioning.py`, replace the plan-review invariant assertion with: `from rebar.llm.plan_review import registry; idx = registry.by_id(); blocking_posture = {k for k, v in idx.items() if v.get('default_posture') == 'blocking'}; assert blocking_posture == KNOWN_PREEXISTING_BLOCKING_SET` where `KNOWN_PREEXISTING_BLOCKING_SET` is the frozen set of criteria IDs that had `default_posture: 'blocking'` before this child (established by reading the current criteria_routing.json).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify (in Section C and AC bullet 2) what `load()` / `main()` does when the filtered corpus is empty — e.g., emit a clear warning + exit non-zero, or at minimum document the minimum-corpus-size precondition — so callers cannot silently miscalibrate on zero data.
findings.finding: The version-filtered `load()` in the calibration replay has no specified behavior when ALL sidecars are filtered out (empty corpus after applying `--impact-model-version`). A caller that passes a valid but not-yet-populated version tag (e.g., `plan-v2` before any tagged sidecars have accumulated) would receive an empty dataset; downstream bucketing, threshold computation, or statistics could silently produce division-by-zero, NaN outputs, or a vacuously 'passing' calibration with no data — none of which are caught or described.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a post-filter guard in `load()`: if the resulting sidecar list is empty, print a message like `"No sidecars matched impact_model_version=<v>; cannot calibrate — aborting"` and either raise or return a sentinel that `main()` converts to a non-zero exit. Add a unit-test case for this in `test_impact_model_versioning.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit decision rule (in Section B or the ADR) specifying what constitutes a `schema` bump vs. an `impact_model_version` bump, and justify why a single-dimension bump to the schema string alone cannot serve the calibration-segmentation purpose.
findings.finding: Section B introduces a second, parallel versioning dimension (`impact_model_version`) alongside the already-established `schema` key (`plan_review_result_v1` / `code_review_result_v1`) without justifying why the existing schema-key versioning mechanism cannot carry the formula-version contract, or documenting the invariant that separates the two dimensions (what changes bump schema vs. what changes bump impact_model_version).
findings.location: Section B — Impact-model versioning; `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py` build_payload; `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` build_payload
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-paragraph note: '`schema` is bumped when the payload's structural shape changes (new/removed top-level keys) in a way that breaks existing readers. `impact_model_version` is bumped when the scoring formula changes but the payload shape is unchanged — a shape-stable, formula-tagged dimension the schema key cannot encode without also invalidating existing readers.' This makes the two axes orthogonal and unambiguous.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the locking-test pseudocode in AC3 to use the actual dict-access API: `{c for c, v in registry.routing_index().items() if v.get('blocking_enabled')} == {'secret-detection', 'high-critical-security'}`, naming `registry.routing_index()` from `rebar.llm.code_review.registry` as the read path.
findings.finding: The permissive-rollout locking test (Section A / AC3) is described with pseudocode `{c for c,v in routing if v.blocking_enabled} == {secret-detection, high-critical-security}` but does not name the API (`registry.routing_index()` from `src/rebar/llm/code_review/registry.py`) needed to read the routing for the assertion, leaving the implementer of `test_impact_model_versioning.py` to rediscover the read path.
findings.location: Section A / AC3: permissive-rollout invariant locking test in 'tests/unit/test_impact_model_versioning.py'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC3 pseudocode with: `from rebar.llm.code_review.registry import routing_index; assert {c for c, v in routing_index().items() if v.get('blocking_enabled')} == {'secret-detection', 'high-critical-security'}`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default for the old-kernel baseline in section D: either name the exact import (`from rebar.llm.code_review.impact import legacy_impact_score` or similar) or explicitly say 'hardcode 0.25 for every row, since the old formula always returns ~0.25 on consequence-binary-only inputs'.
findings.finding: The A/B script (section D) says the old kernel mean score is 'PRINTED for transparency' but never specifies where the old impact model lives (module path, function name) or whether the executor should hardcode ~0.25 (since the plan says the old kernel always returns ~0.25 on these rows). An executor implementing `ab_impact_model.py` must stop and guess the import path or the hardcoded value.
findings.location: Section D — 'The old mean's separation is still PRINTED for transparency'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to section D: 'For the old-baseline print, hardcode `old_baseline_score = 0.25` for every row (the plan documents that the old formula returns a near-constant ~0.25 on consequence-binary inputs); do NOT attempt to import the old kernel.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a direct-invocation proving command for AC 4, e.g. `.venv/bin/python docs/experiments/ab_impact_model.py` (expected exit 0 on the current fixture), alongside the existing pytest command.
findings.finding: There is no standalone proving command that runs `docs/experiments/ab_impact_model.py` as an actual script (e.g. `python docs/experiments/ab_impact_model.py && echo EXIT_0`). All verification for AC 4 routes through `pytest tests/unit/test_impact_model_versioning.py`, which unit-tests the gate logic in isolation. If the script has top-level import errors, an argparse misconfiguration, or a runtime path issue, the unit tests pass while the deliverable script is broken.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC 4's proving commands: 'Direct-run check: `.venv/bin/python docs/experiments/ab_impact_model.py` exits 0 (current fixture satisfies the separation contract).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a named importable function (e.g., `check_separation(score_fn, fixture_path) -> bool`) in `ab_impact_model.py` so `test_impact_model_versioning.py` can import and call it directly with a degenerate constant scorer — satisfying AC4's unit-test requirement without subprocess invocation.
findings.finding: The A/B script `ab_impact_model.py` specifies an EXIT NON-ZERO gate and an AC4 unit test that must prove 'a degenerate constant scorer FAILS', but the plan never defines a callable seam (e.g., a `check_separation(score_fn)` function) that the unit test can import and invoke directly. Without such a seam the test must either spawn a subprocess (fragile, slow, unreliable in CI) or duplicate the gate logic — neither satisfies the 'unit-tested' claim in AC4.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Section D: 'The script exposes a `check_separation(score_fn, fixture_path=DEFAULT_FIXTURE)` function that returns `True` iff the ADR-0035 contract holds; `main()` calls it and exits non-zero on `False`. The unit test imports `check_separation` directly and asserts it returns `False` for `lambda _: 0.5`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `ab_impact_model.py` provides a capability NOT already covered by `test_impact_code_separates_high_from_nit` and `test_impact_code_gap_beats_old_mean_gap`; if the only new value is a human-readable printed report, consider making it a thin wrapper that imports and calls the existing `_median_gap`/fixture helpers from the test module (or a shared utility) rather than re-implementing them.
findings.finding: Section D's `ab_impact_model.py` substantially rebuilds logic that is already pinned as production regression tests in `tests/unit/test_impact_code.py`: the same fixture (`code_review_impact_labels.jsonl`), the same `_median_gap` helper pattern, and the EXACT same absolute gate assertions (`m_nit < 0.30` and `(m_high - m_nit) > 0.30`) already enforced by `test_impact_code_separates_high_from_nit()`. Adding a standalone script that replicates these assertions in a different runner is duplication, not a new capability — the 'regression-detecting gate' the plan advertises already exists and runs in CI.
findings.suggested_fix: Extract `_median_gap` and `_load_fixture` into a shared helper (e.g. `tests/unit/fixtures/impact_fixture_helpers.py` or a `conftest` fixture), then have `ab_impact_model.py` import from it. This eliminates the duplication while keeping the offline-runnable script distinct from pytest.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in Section D whether `ab_impact_model.py` is intended as a standalone CLI tool for human-invoked calibration runs (distinct from pytest) vs. a regression test, and note that `test_impact_code_separates_high_from_nit` and `test_impact_code_gap_beats_old_mean_gap` in `test_impact_code.py` already assert the identical absolute gate — either reference those tests as the authoritative pin or explain the additive value of the new script.
findings.finding: `docs/experiments/ab_impact_model.py` is a new creation that duplicates logic already present in the production test suite: `tests/unit/test_impact_code.py` already contains `_median_gap()`, `test_impact_code_separates_high_from_nit` (asserts `m_nit < 0.30` and `m_high - m_nit > 0.30`), and `test_impact_code_gap_beats_old_mean_gap`. The plan does not classify this as CREATION introducing new scope vs. already-covered behavior, nor does it explain how the standalone script's gate differs from the pinned production test.
findings.location: Section D — 'Add a NEW offline A/B script docs/experiments/ab_impact_model.py'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify the additive value of `ab_impact_model.py` over the already-pinned production tests (`test_impact_code_separates_high_from_nit`, `test_impact_code_gap_beats_old_mean_gap`), or narrow the script's scope to what the tests cannot provide (e.g., corpus-scale offline scoring of live sidecars rather than re-running the fixture).
findings.finding: Section D proposes a new standalone `docs/experiments/ab_impact_model.py` that gates on the ADR-0035 absolute separation contract (`median(HIGH) - median(NIT) > 0.30` AND `median(NIT) < 0.30`) on the `code_review_impact_labels.jsonl` fixture — logic already implemented and pinned as production regression tests (`test_impact_code_separates_high_from_nit` and `test_impact_code_gap_beats_old_mean_gap` in `tests/unit/test_impact_code.py`). The plan does not justify the additive value of repackaging existing CI-pinned tests into a separate offline script.
findings.location: Section D — Diff-grounded A/B harness; `docs/experiments/ab_impact_model.py`; AC line 4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the separation-contract thresholds as named module-level constants (e.g., `MIN_SEPARATION = 0.30`, `MAX_NIT_MEDIAN = 0.30`) or CLI-overridable parameters at the top of `ab_impact_model.py` so a future ADR revision requires only one canonical edit and the constants are visibly co-located with their ADR reference.
findings.finding: The ADR-0035 separation thresholds hardcoded in `ab_impact_model.py` (`median(HIGH) - median(NIT) > 0.30` AND `median(NIT) < 0.30`) are embedded as literals in script logic rather than as named, top-level constants or CLI-overridable parameters. The plan's own cadence (Section E) anticipates future threshold-down proposals; when ADR-0035 is revised, a developer must locate and edit the assertion deep in the script body rather than updating a single named constant — a silent drift risk.
findings.suggested_fix:
At the top of `ab_impact_model.py` add:
```python
# ADR-0035 separation contract — update here when ADR is revised
MIN_HIGH_NIT_SEPARATION = 0.30
MAX_NIT_MEDIAN = 0.30
```
Replace the inline literals with these constants in the gate condition, and optionally expose them as `--min-separation` / `--max-nit-median` CLI args for the unit test's degenerate-scorer case.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the five existing `build_payload` callers (`test_sidecar_enrichment.py`, `test_code_review_ws4.py`, `test_plan_review.py`, `test_code_review_artifact.py`, `workflow/test_plan_review_metrics.py`) in the scope section as acknowledged (non-breaking) consumers, confirming none perform exact-key-set validation that would reject the new `impact_model_version` field.
findings.finding: Five existing test files outside the plan's named scope call `build_payload` on one or both sidecar modules and assert on the returned dict's structure, but none are enumerated as consumers to verify against the new `impact_model_version` key. While the addition is additive and existing assertions won't break, the plan gives no acknowledgement that these consumers exist.
findings.location: Section B / Scope & Files table — 'Both are additive: existing sidecar readers ignore an unknown key'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note under Section B: 'Verified external callers of `build_payload` in `test_sidecar_enrichment.py`, `test_code_review_ws4.py`, `test_plan_review.py`, `test_code_review_artifact.py`, and `workflow/test_plan_review_metrics.py` perform only positive-key assertions and will not fail on the additive new key; no changes to those files required.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `load()` is extended to `load(version_filter: str | None = None)` (a keyword parameter defaulting to `None` for back-compat), and `main()` passes `args.impact_model_version` to it — pinning the threading mechanism so the AC2 unit test can call `load(version_filter='plan-v2')` directly without argparse.
findings.finding: The plan specifies threading `--impact-model-version` from argparse in `main()` into `load()`, but does not specify the threading mechanism. The current `load()` takes no parameters; the plan leaves open whether the version filter should be passed as a function parameter, captured in a closure, or stored in a module-level variable — each has different implications for test isolation and back-compat.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Section C: 'Change `load()` to `load(version_filter: str | None = None) -> dict[str, list[dict]]:`; `main()` passes `version_filter=args.impact_model_version`. The unit test calls `load(version_filter=...)` directly — no argparse needed in the test.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
